afd_data.text:
.AVIATION...
(18Z TAFS)
Issued at 1010 AM PDT Sun Mar 29 2026

Currently VFR at all terminals with high clouds streaming in and
stratus remaining confined to the Big Sur Coast. Moderate to high
confidence on VFR prevailing through the TAF period. Tonight another
southerly surge may try again and conditions will cool and moisten
with an approaching cold front, but confidence is low that sub-VFR
conditions will develop at the terminals as a result. Diurnal winds
will prevail. Haze will reduce slant range visibilities.

Vicinity of SFO...Currently VFR with light northeasterly flow. High
confidence in VFR through the TAF period. Winds will back through
the TAF period, maintaining diurnal patterns.

SFO Bridge Approach...Similar to SFO.

Monterey Bay Terminals...Currently VFR and calm at MRY and VFR with
drainage flow at SNS. Moderate confidence on VFR prevailing through
the TAF period. A southerly surge may try again tonight, but
confidence is low that it`ll impact the terminals. Even if the
terminals aren`t directly impacted, low ceilings/visibilities should
be expected within the area, especially along the Big Sur Coast.
Diurnal winds will prevail.

.AVIATION /00Z TAFS THROUGH 00Z TUESDAY/...
Issued at 528 PM MDT Sun Mar 29 2026

VFR conditions are expected at KALS, KCOS, and KPUB through the next
24 hours. Breezy winds will linger at KALS and KPUB for a few more
hours, before winds weaken overnight. Mid level cloud cover will
increase through most of this forecast period as well. By 18Z on
Monday, gusty west winds will set in across the area, with gusts 25-
30 knots expected at all three terminals into Monday evening.

prompts.system:
You are an experienced CFII/charter pilot briefing another pilot.
Be direct, practical, and evidence-based. Write in plain pilot language.

CORE RULES

1) Evidence discipline
Only describe a hazard as affecting the flight if it overlaps the route, time window, and altitude band.
Do not overstate localized or uncertain conditions.
Use:
- likely = strong direct support
- possible / conditional = limited or localized support
- low confidence = weak or missing support

2) Icing rule
Icing requires both subfreezing temperatures and moisture/cloud evidence.
Do not infer icing from temperature alone.
If icing probability is low or moisture is absent, say icing is not a significant factor.
If no SLD signal exists, say so explicitly.

3) Terrain rule
Distinguish large-scale weather from terrain-driven effects.
If terrain effects are expected, explain the operational impact briefly:
departure, en route crossing, arrival.
Do not describe terrain effects as route-wide unless guidance supports it.

4) Decision framework
At the beginning, state:
- what kind of day this is
- the primary operational risk
- GO / MARGINAL GO / NO-GO
- the single most important trigger that would change the decision

For the primary risk, give 1-3 practical decision triggers, with thresholds where possible.

5) AFD rule
Use AFD only as supporting context.
It may:
- increase or decrease confidence
- highlight forecaster concern or uncertainty
It may not:
- introduce hazards unsupported by forecast data
- override chart evidence

6) Forecast maturity
If more than 48 hours out, treat confidence as limited.
24-48 hours out: moderate confidence unless data strongly agrees.
Within 24 hours: be as decisive as the evidence allows.

INPUT PRIORITY
Use, in order:
1. TAFs / surface forecast
2. winds aloft
3. icing / turbulence / cloud / QPF products
4. G-AIRMET / SIGMET / SigWx
5. AFD for confidence adjustment

Charts showing no meaningful weather impact along the route go in the reference pile. G-AIRMET Icing +00hr (valid Sun 18Z) - G-AIRMET IFR/Mtn Obscn +12hr (valid Mon 06Z) - G-AIRMET Turb/LLWS +12hr (valid Mon 06Z) - G-AIRMET Freezing +12hr (valid Mon 06Z) - G-AIRMET Icing +12hr (valid Mon 06Z) - GFA Clouds +03hr (valid Sun 21Z) - GFA Surface +03hr (valid Sun 21Z) - GFA Clouds +15hr (valid Mon 09Z) - GFA Surface +15hr (valid Mon 09Z) - GFA Clouds +18hr (valid Mon 12Z) - GFA Surface +18hr (valid Mon 12Z) - TCF +04hr (valid Sun 22Z) - TCF +08hr (valid Mon 02Z) - ETCF +10hr (valid Mon 04Z) - ETCF +14hr (valid Mon 08Z) - ETCF +16hr (valid Mon 10Z) - SIGMET Current (valid Sun 18Z) - SigWx Low +00hr (valid Sun 00Z) - SigWx Low +12hr (valid Mon 00Z) - SigWx Low +18hr (valid Mon 12Z) THEN a blank line, then the pilot briefing below. --- REQUIRED SECTIONS (use these exact h2 headings):

Executive Summary

One short paragraph with exactly: a) What kind of day this is (e.g., "This is a VMC wind/terrain day, not a weather system problem.") b) Primary Risk: one sentence c) GO / MARGINAL GO / NO-GO d) The single most important trigger to monitor Do not expand beyond these four elements. A pilot should read only this and know the call.

Route Hazards

Hazard table covering the entire route — a quick-scan reference before reading details. Hazard | Risk | Leg | What to Expect Incorporate icing (FIP), turbulence (GTG), G-AIRMET, SIGMET, SigWx, and QPF findings. If no significant hazards exist, say so in one line instead of an empty table.

The Day Before — What to Watch

Short practical re-check list for the evening before. What to look for and what would change the go/no-go.

Leaving KSQL — Departure at 2026-03-30 15:00Z

Describe departure ceiling/visibility/wind. State whether this is: - VMC departure - brief layer penetration - likely departure IMC (only if TAFs show BKN/OVC at or below climb altitudes) Use pilot language, not METAR codes.

The Ride at 17,000 ft

WINDS ALOFT: If winds/temps data is provided, decode and present actual winds at cruise altitude for stations along the route. Calculate headwind/tailwind/crosswind components. RIDE QUALITY: Classify using standard terms only: smooth / light chop / light turbulence / moderate turbulence / severe turbulence Wind-over-terrain reference: - <20 kt: minimal terrain effects - 20-30 kt: light to moderate terrain turbulence possible - 30-40 kt: mountain wave likely, moderate turbulence possible - >40 kt: moderate or greater turbulence likely ICING: Include a vertical icing table: Altitude | Icing Prob | Notes State: freezing level, worst icing band, SLD risk (or explicitly no SLD signal). ALTITUDE STRATEGY: State explicitly: a) best altitude band and why b) worst altitude band and why c) escape option if conditions worsen: climb, descend, divert, or turn around

Getting Into KCOS

Describe expected arrival conditions. Estimate block time. Include: runway wind estimate with uncertainty, practical alternates. Distinguish: non-event VMC arrival / terrain-localized issue / broader weather issue. For terrain airports: do not assume winds aloft = runway winds. State uncertainty explicitly.

Fly or No

Verdict first: GO / MARGINAL GO / NO-GO. Hard stops, then watch items. If recommending IFR, state why: IMC, terrain complexity, or workload/safety margin.

What changes this call

- 2-4 bullets max - at least one quantitative trigger (winds > X kt, ceilings < X ft)

If You Go — Do This

Concrete action items only: altitude, alternates, fuel, what to check morning-of. If departure timing materially changes risk (convection, frontal passage, diurnal winds, marine layer), state best and worst windows with a one-sentence reason each. If timing is not a factor, omit.

Confidence

State High / Medium / Low and what forecast elements could still change the call. Do not assign High if terrain-driven effects dominate or critical data is missing. FACTUAL ACCURACY: Do not guess airport characteristics (towered/non-towered, airspace class, runway count, elevation) unless you are certain. If unsure, omit rather than state incorrectly. Use only the airport names and data provided in the FLIGHT header. Respond with HTML using: h2, h3, h4, p, ul, ol, li, strong, em, blockquote, table, thead, tbody, tr, th, td, code, hr. No html/head/body/style/script tags.

briefing_html:
Executive Summary

This is a wind and high-terrain day, not a frontal-weather problem — the primary system is well north of the route, and the KSQL departure and en-route corridor are VMC. The primary risk is building westerly winds and moderate turbulence over the Colorado Rockies during the arrival window at KCOS, with gusty surface winds (15G25 kt forecast by 18Z) and potentially lowering ceilings to BKN100. Decision: MARGINAL GO for an early-afternoon departure; winds and turbulence tighten significantly by late afternoon. The single most important trigger is the KCOS TAF FM301800Z wind/ceiling call — if winds are already gusting 25+ kt and ceilings are already at or below BKN100 at your ETA of ~20Z, evaluate an early departure or delay until the next morning.

Route Hazards

Hazard Risk Leg What to Expect
Mountain wave / turbulence over Sierra Nevada Light–Moderate KSQL → NV border (~16,000–17,000 ft) Westerly flow 25–30 kt at cruise; possible light chop over Sierra crest. GTG shows yellow (light-moderate) over CA/NV border at FL180.
Turbulence — southern Rockies / KCOS arrival Moderate, possible CO border → KCOS G-AIRMET TurbHi 240–420 valid Mon 09Z over northern Rockies. GTG +18hr shows moderate (orange) over central Rockies at FL180. West winds building to 15G25 kt at surface by 18–20Z.
Icing at cruise altitude (17,000 ft) Low En route FIP icing probability at FL180 is near-zero or trace along the CA/NV/UT corridor by +18hr. Freezing level well below cruise. No SLD signal anywhere on route. Not a significant factor at 17,000 ft for this flight.
Icing below 12,000 ft (climb/descent) Low–possible KCOS descent FL120 icing probability near-zero over Colorado at +18hr. Freezing level at KCOS area is near surface (cold high pressure, elevated terrain). Clouds are mid-level (BKN100–140 at KCOS). Watch for trace icing in the cloud layer during descent.
KCOS arrival winds / gusty crosswind Moderate KCOS arrival (~20Z) TAF: FM301800Z 27015G25KT, BKN100. West/northwest gusts to 25 kt. KCOS has N/S and NW/SE runways — runway 35R or 17L (ILS) into the wind, but gust spread and mountain rotor possible.
KCOS ceilings Low–moderate KCOS arrival TAF BKN100 forecast at 18Z. MVFR possible. Not IFR but worth watching. SCT140/SCT250 precedes the deterioration — the BKN100 is the trailing wind/cloud regime.
Convection None All TCF/ETCF show no convective criteria anywhere on route during the flight window. Not a factor.
KSQL departure weather Nil Departure KSFO TAF: VFR, BKN/OVC250, westerly winds 5–12 kt. Clean departure expected.

The Day Before — What to Watch

  • Check the KCOS TAF update Sunday evening. The critical question is when the FM301800Z BKN100 / 27015G25 wind regime arrives. If it trends earlier (e.g., 15Z instead of 18Z), that eats into your arrival buffer.
  • Look at PIREPs over the Colorado Rockies (Leadville, Gunnison, Pueblo corridor) for turbulence reports Monday morning. If moderate-or-greater turbulence PIREPs appear at FL160–200 over southern Colorado, consider bumping your departure time earlier or later.
  • Watch the G-AIRMET TurbHi update. The Mon 09Z G-AIRMET already calls TurbHi 240–420 over the northern Rockies. If the next issuance extends that southward into southern Colorado/KCOS area, that changes the ride quality assessment from possible to likely moderate.
  • Decision trigger: If KCOS TAF deteriorates to OVC080 or lower, or winds forecast to exceed 25G35 kt by your ETA, this becomes a NO-GO or delay until Tuesday morning when winds calm behind the system.

Leaving KSQL — Departure at 2026-03-30 15:00Z

This is a straightforward VMC departure. The KSFO TAF is solid: winds 280°/12 kt at the start of your flight, VFR, with a broken-to-overcast layer at FL250 — that's high cirrus, not a factor for departure. No precipitation, no low ceilings. Expect the standard Bay Area diurnal pattern: afternoon sea breeze establishing from the west-northwest, which means you'll likely depart runway 30 at KSQL with a manageable headwind/crosswind. Noise abatement in effect — check the AMGR-published procedures. The AFD notes haze may reduce slant-range visibility, but ceiling and flight visibility are VFR throughout.

Expect a clean climb eastbound through the Coast Range and over the Central Valley. No IFR layer penetration required.

The Ride at 17,000 ft

Winds Aloft

Data valid 30/06Z (your flight is 15–20Z Monday; these winds will be representative of mid-flight conditions, trending stronger by arrival):

Station Location FL180 Wind Temp Component at 17,000 ft (est.)
SFO San Francisco (departure) 240°/25 kt −14°C Crosswind departure; light tailwind eastbound
LAS Las Vegas (mid-route) 240°/17 kt −14°C Light quartering tailwind
PUB Pueblo (near KCOS) 240°/12 kt −14°C Light crosswind at arrival
DEN Denver (north of KCOS) 230°/20 kt −15°C Light quartering tailwind from the west

Cruise track KSQL → KCOS is roughly 075°–085° magnetic. The 240° winds give you a light quartering tailwind component — expect a small groundspeed boost overall. No significant headwind. Note these are the 06Z forecast winds; by 15–18Z Monday winds will likely be slightly stronger per the surface prog trend, but the direction should hold.

Ride Quality

KSQL to Nevada border: Light chop likely over the Sierra Nevada crest. Westerly flow at 25 kt is in the 20–30 kt range over terrain — light to moderate terrain turbulence possible near the crest. Expect smooth to light chop in the Central Valley and eastern Sierra approach, with the chop concentrated over the crest itself.

Nevada / Utah corridor: Smooth to light chop. No significant terrain or jet-stream interaction. GTG +15hr shows green (light) over NV/UT at FL180.

Colorado Rockies / KCOS approach: This is the focus. GTG +18hr shows moderate (orange) at FL180 over the central/southern Rockies. G-AIRMET TurbHi 240–420 is in effect over northern Colorado. At 17,000 ft you're below the TurbHi band (which starts at FL240) but within range of terrain-induced moderate turbulence below. Expect light to moderate turbulence over the Rockies as you begin descent, with the worst of it likely between Gunnison and KCOS (the typical leeside turbulence band). By your ETA (~20Z), surface winds are gusting to 25 kt at KCOS — mountain wave activity is likely.

Icing

Altitude Icing Probability Notes
17,000 ft (cruise) Near-zero / trace FIP +18hr shows essentially no icing probability at FL180 along the CA/NV/UT/CO route. Temperatures at −14 to −15°C. No cloud moisture at cruise altitude en route.
12,000–15,000 ft (descent into KCOS) Low (trace possible) FIP +18hr FL120/FL150 shows very low probability over Colorado. BKN100–140 deck at KCOS could have trace icing. Freezing level at KCOS is at or near surface (elevated terrain, cold high).
Below 10,000 ft (KCOS approach) Possible/conditional In the BKN100 cloud layer, trace icing possible. Not forecast as a significant hazard but be aware on final.

Freezing level: Near surface at KCOS (elevation 6,187 ft MSL, cold high pressure). En route over NV/UT, freezing level is around FL040–FL080 per G-AIRMET freezing level contours. At cruise altitude (17,000 ft) you're well above the freezing level everywhere but moisture is absent — icing is not a significant factor at cruise altitude. No SLD signal anywhere on the route for this time period.

Altitude Strategy

Best altitude: 17,000 ft is appropriate for the cruise segment through Nevada and Utah — above terrain turbulence, light quartering tailwind. Consider 15,000 ft if you want to stay below the more significant turbulence bands over the Rockies, though you'll be in potential cloud layer on the KCOS approach.

Worst altitude band: FL240+ over the central Rockies during your arrival period — G-AIRMET TurbHi starts at FL240. You're below that at 17,000, which is actually a better position than higher altitudes for this route.

Escape option: If turbulence is unacceptable over the Rockies, descend to 14,000–15,000 ft (terrain permitting — MEA in that corridor; know your route MEAs before departure). Divert to KPUB (Pueblo) — similar elevation but slightly more sheltered from westerly mountain wave. Do not attempt to climb into the TurbHi band above FL240.

Getting Into KCOS

Expected ETA: ~20Z Monday (approximately 5 hours block time SFO to COS distance, including climb/descent).

The KCOS TAF tells the story clearly:

  • At your ETA (~20Z): FM301800Z 27015G25KT P6SM BKN100 — winds 270° at 15, gusting 25 kt; broken ceiling at 10,000 ft AGL (16,187 ft MSL). That's MVFR ceilings, not IFR, but it's the bottom of the acceptable VFR range and trending the wrong way.
  • Earlier in the day (14Z–18Z): SCT140 SCT250 — better conditions, higher ceilings.

Runway winds at KCOS around 20Z: 270°/15G25 kt. KCOS's long runway 17L/35R (ILS both ends) is north-south — a 270° wind gives you a direct crosswind on that runway. Runway 13/31 (8,270 ft) aligns better with 270° winds — runway 31 would give you roughly a direct headwind/slight crosswind. That's your likely active runway. Gust spread of 10 kt is manageable but plan for firm, controlled approaches. Mountain rotor effects cannot be ruled out — don't let airspeed bleed on final.

Terrain note: Pikes Peak and the Front Range are immediately to the west of KCOS. In 270° flow at 15G25 kt, expect turbulence on final approach from the west. Increase Vref by half the gust factor (5 kt additive) as a minimum.

Practical alternates:

  • KPUB (Pueblo) — 40 nm southeast, lower elevation (4,726 ft MSL), often sheltered from the worst of the mountain wave. Your best alternate.
  • KDEN (Denver) — 70 nm north, major airport, multiple runways, good approaches. Further from the mountain wave. Secondary alternate.

File with legal alternate fuel. If ceilings drop below 1,500 ft AGL (ceiling below 1,100 ft MSL + 6,187 ft elev = ~7,287 ft) or winds exceed your aircraft's demonstrated crosswind component, go to KPUB.

Fly or No

MARGINAL GO — the flight is doable, but the arrival window is the variable that makes or breaks it.

Hard stops:

  • KCOS TAF degrades to OVC080 or below at your ETA → NO-GO or divert-plan only
  • Winds at KCOS exceeding demonstrated crosswind component for your aircraft (runway 13/31 crosswind on 270° flow)
  • PIREPs of severe turbulence over the southern Rockies at your cruise altitude

Watch items:

  • KCOS TAF trend morning-of — is the BKN100/27015G25 timing shifting earlier?
  • PIREPs from the Denver/Pueblo/Colorado Springs sector Monday afternoon
  • Whether the G-AIRMET TurbHi extends southward into the KCOS corridor

If IFR rated: Fly IFR. The KCOS approach environment (mountain rotor, gusty crosswind, MVFR ceilings) benefits enormously from instrument proficiency and IFR currency. ILS 17L or ILS 35R is available. This is not a day for a visual approach in gusty crosswind conditions if you can avoid it.

What changes this call

  • KCOS winds exceed 25G35 kt or crosswind component exceeds aircraft limits → NO-GO or divert to KPUB from top of descent
  • KCOS ceiling drops to OVC080 or below at your ETA → NO-GO unless IFR current and proficient, with solid alternate
  • PIREPs of moderate-or-greater turbulence below FL200 over southern Colorado on Monday afternoon → reconsider altitude strategy and timing; if sustained severe reported, NO-GO
  • Earlier departure (12Z–13Z) catches KCOS in the SCT140 window before the BKN100/gusts establish → converts this to a straightforward GO

If You Go — Do This

  1. Depart by 13:00Z if possible — arrival around 18Z catches KCOS in the SCT140/SCT250 pre-wind window. The 15Z departure targets an ~20Z arrival right at the leading edge of the worst conditions. Earlier is better.
  2. Worst departure window: after 15Z. Every hour later means more wind and lower ceilings at KCOS. If you can't get wheels up by 14Z, seriously consider delaying until Tuesday morning when the pattern relaxes.
  3. Fuel for KPUB alternate — file it, carry the fuel. KPUB is 40 nm southeast and will likely have better conditions if KCOS goes below minimums.
  4. File IFR. KCOS at 6,187 ft MSL with BKN100 AGL, gusty crosswinds, and mountain terrain is not a place to be troubleshooting alternates on a VFR flight plan.
  5. Morning-of checks (by 12Z Monday):
    • Updated KCOS TAF — confirm BKN100/wind timing
    • PIREPs over CO — any MOG/SEV reports below FL200?
    • Updated G-AIRMET for turbulence extent
    • Check PIREP boards for SFO–LAS corridor (Sierra wave activity)
  6. Add 5 kt to Vref on the KCOS approach for the gust factor, and expect turbulence on short final from the west.
  7. Know your go-around plan. If you don't have it stabilized by 500 ft AGL, go around. Don't chase a bouncy approach at a field with terrain and gusty conditions.

Confidence

Medium confidence overall.

  • The KSQL departure and en-route corridor are high confidence — VMC, light winds, well-described by the TAF and surface prog.
  • The KCOS arrival timing is medium confidence — the TAF has a sharp transition at FM301800Z, but the exact onset of BKN100 and 15G25 winds could be 1–2 hours earlier or later. That timing window is the entire margin of this flight.
  • The turbulence intensity over the Rockies is medium-to-low confidence — GTG and G-AIRMET agree on the area but not the intensity. Mountain wave turbulence in this corridor can be worse than models suggest when the flow is strong and well-organized.
  • What could change the call: a morning KCOS TAF showing earlier wind onset, or PIREPs reporting moderate turbulence below FL200 over southern Colorado. Either would push this toward NO-GO for the 15Z departure slot.
